verses.sec: Śīkṣāvallī — Phonetics and Disciplines (Chapter I)
verses.dev: ॐ शं नो मित्रः शं वरुणः । शं नो भवत्यर्यमा । शं न इन्द्रो बृहस्पतिः । शं नो विष्णुरुरुक्रमः । नमो ब्रह्मणे । नमस्ते वायो । त्वमेव प्रत्यक्षं ब्रह्मासि । त्वामेव प्रत्यक्षं ब्रह्म वदिष्यामि । ऋतं वदिष्यामि । सत्यं वदिष्यामि । तन्मामवतु । तद्वक्तारमवतु । अवतु माम् । अवतु वक्तारम् । ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: oṃ śaṃ no mitraḥ śaṃ varuṇaḥ | śaṃ no bhavatyaryamā | śaṃ na indro bṛhaspatiḥ | śaṃ no viṣṇururukramaḥ | namo brahmaṇe | namaste vāyo | tvameva pratyakṣaṃ brahmāsi | tvāmeva pratyakṣaṃ brahmā vadiṣyāmi | ṛtaṃ vadiṣyāmi | satyaṃ vadiṣyāmi | tanmāmavatu | tadvaktāramavatu | avatu mām | avatu वक्तारम् | oṃ śāntiḥ śāntiḥ śāntiḥ || 1 ||
verses.trans: May Mitra be propitious to us, and Varuna be propitious to us. May Aryaman be propitious to us. May Indra and Brihaspati be propitious to us. May Vishnu of wide strides be propitious to us. Salutation to Brahman. Salutation to thee, O Vayu. Thou indeed art the perceptible Brahman. Thee alone I shall declare as the perceptible Brahman. I shall speak of the Right (Ritam). I shall speak of the Truth (Satyam). May That protect me. May That protect the teacher. May It protect me, may It protect the teacher. Om, Peace, Peace, Peace.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara notes that the propitiation of cosmic deities (Mitra ruling prāna, Varuna ruling apāna, etc.) is necessary to remove internal and cosmic impediments to spiritual studies. Vayu is declared as 'perceptible Brahman' because Vayu manifests as the life-force (Prāna) which is immediately accessible and observable within the body, representing the cosmic thread (Sūtrātman) of reality.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that these cosmic deities are not independent lords but form the collective spiritual body of the Supreme Lord (Parabrahman), who acts as their ultimate inner controller (Antaryāmin). Vayu is praised because the vital breath is a direct expression of the Lord’s life-sustaining power in the universe.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya maintains a strict dualism. The deities are finite jivas appointed by the Lord to planetary administrative offices. Vayu holds the unique position of Mukhya-Prāna, the highest of all created beings and the direct mediator between the soul and Sri Hari. Calling Vayu 'Brahman' indicates his absolute alignment with and reflection of God's will, not essential identity.

verses.sec: Śīkṣāvallī — Phonetics and Disciplines (Chapter I)
verses.dev: ॐ शीक्षां व्याख्यास्यामः । वर्णः स्वरः । मात्रा बलम् । साम सन्तानः । इत्युक्तः शीक्षाध्यायः ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: oṃ śīkṣāṃ vyākhyāsyāmaḥ | varṇaḥ svaraḥ | mātrā balam | sāma santānaḥ | ityuktaḥ śīkṣādhyāyaḥ || 1 ||
verses.trans: We shall now explain the science of Phonetics: sounds/letters, pitch/accent, quantity/duration, force of articulation, modulation/evenness, and combinations. Thus is declared the chapter on phonetics.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara comments that accurate pronunciation of the Vedic text is vital because speech is a sacred instrument. Discipline over physical speech and phonetics serves as the psychological foundation for tuning the intellect to grasp the subtle, non-physical realities of the Self later on.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The tradition emphasizes that correct articulation ensures the preservation of the vibrational purity of the Vedic words. Sound is a divine attribute of space created by Ishvara, and using it correctly is an act of deep reverence.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva highlights that every letter and accent has a specific, divinely ordained meaning. Altering the sound changes the semantic meaning and disrupts the alignment with the specific forms of the Lord intended by the mantra.

verses.sec: Śīkṣāvallī — Phonetics and Disciplines (Chapter I)
verses.dev: सह नौ यशः । सह नौ ब्रह्मवर्चसम् । अथातः संहिताया उपनिषदं व्याख्यास्यामः । पञ्चस्वधिकरणेषु । अळिलोकमधिज्यौतिषमधिविद्यमधिप्रजमध्यात्मम् । ता महासंहिता इत्याचक्षते । ... ॥ १-६ ॥
verses.iast: saha nau yaśaḥ | saha nau brahmavarcasam | athātaḥ saṃhitāyā upaniṣadaṃ vyākhyāsyāmaḥ | pañcasvadhikaraṇeṣu | adhilokamadhijyautiṣamadhividyamadhiprajamadhyātmam | tā mahāsaṃhitā ityācakṣate | ... || 1-6 ||
verses.trans: May glory come to both of us (teacher and student) together. May the light of sacred knowledge belong to both of us. Now we shall explain the secret doctrine of combinations under five headings: concerning the worlds, the luminous bodies, knowledge, progeny, and the self. These are called the Great Conjunctions.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara notes that because immature minds cannot immediately leap to the absolute abstract Brahman, the Upanishad provides Upāsanās (meditations) using familiar structural duos (like Earth and Sky, Teacher and Student) to expand individual consciousness into cosmic consciousness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: These five cosmic combinations demonstrate the deep, interconnected organic structure of creation, where every domain—from astronomy to biology—is woven together as the multi-layered body of the Supreme Controller.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets the conjunctions as the intentional structural dependencies of the universe. The matching forces (like fire and sun) are distinct entities ruled by specific subordinate deities, all acting under the sovereign direction of Vishnu.

verses.sec: Śīkṣāvallī — Phonetics and Disciplines (Chapter I)
verses.dev: यश्छन्दसामुषभो विश्वरूपः । छन्दोभ्योऽध्यमृतात्सम्बभूव । स मेन्द्रो मेधया स्पृणोतु । अमृतस्य देवधारणो भूयासम् । शरीरं मे विचर्षणम् । जिह्वा मे मधुमत्तमा । कर्णाभ्यां भूरि विश्रुवम् । ब्रह्मणः कोशोऽसि मेधया पिहितः । श्रुतं मे गोपाय । ... ॥ १-३ ॥
verses.iast: yaśchandasāmuṣabho viśvarūpaḥ | chandobhyo'dhyamṛtātsambabhūva | sa mendro medhayā spṛṇotu | amṛtasya devadhāraṇo bhūyāsam | śarīraṃ me vicarṣaṇam | jihvā me madhumattamā | karṇābhyāṃ bhūri viśruvam | brahmaṇaḥ kośo'si medhayā pihitaḥ | śrutaṃ me gopāya | ... || 1-3 ||
verses.trans: May He who is the bull of the Vedic verses, who assumes all forms, who came into being from the immortal Vedas—may that Supreme Lord (Indra/Om) strengthen me with wisdom. O Divine One, may I become a vessel of immortality. May my body be vigorous, my tongue sweet, and my ears hear much. You are the sheath of Brahman, veiled by worldly intellect. Protect my learning.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara defines 'Indra' here as the sacred syllable OM, the lord of all speech. This prayer is not for mere material wealth, but for a sharp, retentive memory (Medhā) and healthy physical senses, without which the rigorous study of Vedānta is practically impossible.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The 'bull of the Vedas' is identified as Paravāsu-deva. The student prays for the divine grace of a purified mind and body, transforming the physical frame into a worthy temple capable of retaining sublime spiritual insights.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva glosses Indra here as the Supreme Lord Himself, the giver of strength. The phrase 'sheath of Brahman' refers to the mind which contains the memory of God; the prayer asks that this mental vessel not be corrupted by false material philosophies.

verses.sec: Śīkṣāvallī — Phonetics and Disciplines (Chapter I)
verses.dev: भूर्भुवः सुवरिति वा एतास्तिस्रो व्याहृतयः । तासामु ह स्मैतां चतुर्थीम् । माहाचमस्यः प्रवेदयते । मह इति । तद्ब्रह्म । स आत्मा । अङ्गान्यन्या देवताः । ... ॥ १-४ ॥
verses.iast: bhūrbhuvaḥ suvariti vā etāstisro vyāhṛtayaḥ | tāsāmu ha smaitāṃ caturthīm | māhācamasyaḥ pravedayate | maha iti | tadbrahma | sa ātmā | aṅgānyanyā devatāḥ | ... || 1-4 ||
verses.trans: Bhūh, Bhuvah, Suvah—these are the three mystical utterances. In addition to them, the son of Mahācamasa discovered a fourth, namely, Mahah. That is Brahman; that is the Self. The other gods are its limbs.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that Bhūh is this world, Bhuvah is the mid-region, and Suvah is heaven, while Mahah is the Sun/Brahman that illumines them all. This ritual framework guides the mind to see the macrocosm nested inside a unified cosmic reality.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The three lower worlds are states of finite cosmic matter, while Mahah represents the radiant presence of the Lord illuminating them from within. The individual deities governing these spheres function dependently like limbs of a body.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the four utterances correspond to four forms of the Lord: Pradyumna, Aniruddha, Saṅkarṣaṇa, and Vāsudeva. The soul meditates on these forms to recognize that every region of space is supervised by the Divine.

verses.sec: Śīkṣāvallī — Phonetics and Disciplines (Chapter I)
verses.dev: स य एषोऽन्तर्हृदय आकाशः । तस्मिन्नयं पुरुषो मनोमयः । अमृतो हिरण्मयः । अन्तरेण तालुके । य एष स्तन इवावलम्बते । सेन्द्रयोनिः । यत्रासौ केशान्तो विवर्तते । व्यपोह्य शीर्षकपाले । ... ॥ १-२ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya eṣo'ntarhṛdaya ākāśaḥ | tasminnayaṃ puruṣo manomayaḥ | amṛto hiraṇmayaḥ | antareṇa tāluke | ya eṣa stana ivāvalambate | sendrayoniḥ | yatrāsau keśānto vivartate | vyapohya śīrṣakapāle | ... || 1-2 ||
verses.trans: Within the space inside the heart resides the Purusha, consisting of mind, immortal and golden. Passing through the midspace of the palate, right through that which hangs down like a nipple (the uvula), and emerging where the roots of the hair split open the skull—that is the path of Indra (Brahman).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that 'consisting of mind' means Brahman is perceived by a purified mind. This verse outlines Saguṇa Upāsanā combined with the yogic path of exit via the Suṣumṇā Nādī at the time of death, leading to gradual liberation (Krama-Mukti).
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The golden Purusha is the personal Lord in His subtle form, residing in the micro-space of the devotee's heart. The path through the crown of the head is the actual spiritual exit route granted by divine grace to a devoted yogi.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva clarifies that 'golden' means possessing auspicious, luminous attributes, not composed of material gold. The Lord sits in the heart to guide the jiva's internal mental faculties, and frees the soul via this upper channel upon cosmic release.

verses.sec: Śīkṣāvallī — Phonetics and Disciplines (Chapter I)
verses.dev: पृथिव्यन्तरिक्षं द्यौर्दिशोऽवान्तरदिशः । अग्निर्वायुरादित्यश्चन्द्रमा नक्षत्राणि । आप ओषधयो वनस्पतय आकाश आत्मा । इत्यधिभूतम् । अथाध्यात्मम् । प्राणो व्यानोऽपान उदानः समानः । चक्षुः श्रोत्रं मनो वाक् त्वक् । चर्म माँसँ स्नावोस्थि मज्जा । एतदधिविधाय ऋषिरावचत् । पाङ्क्तं वा इदँ सर्वम् । पाङ्क्तेनैव पाङ्क्तँ स्पृणोतीति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: pṛthivyaṅtarikṣaṃ dyaurdiśo'vāntaradiśah | agnirvāyurādityaścandramā nakṣatrāṇi | āpa oṣadhayo vanaspataya ākāśa ātmā | ityādhibhūtam | athādhyātmam | prāṇo vyāno'pāna udānaḥ samānaḥ | cakṣuḥ śrotraṃ mano vāk tvak | carma mā̃sã snāvōsthi majjā | etadadhividhāya ṛṣirāvacat | pāṅktaṃ vā idã sarvam | pāṅktenaiva pāṅktã spṛṇōtīti || 1 ||
verses.trans: Earth, mid-region, heaven, quarters, and intermediate quarters; fire, air, sun, moon, and stars; water, herbs, trees, space, and the body—this is with regard to external objects (Adhibhuta). Now with regard to the individual self (Adhyatma): Prana, Vyana, Apana, Udana, and Samana; eye, ear, mind, speech, and touch; skin, flesh, muscle, bone, and marrow. Having ordained this, the Rishi said: 'All this universe is fivefold. By the fivefold individual, one fills and satisfies the fivefold cosmic.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara notes that this meditation utilizes the concept of 'Pāṅkta' (groups of five, matching the sacred Pankti metre). By consciously correlating the five external elements/cosmic layers with the five physiological systems of the individual body, the seeker breaks the illusion of isolation, understanding that the microcosm and the macrocosm are structurally woven from the same thread of Consciousness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja points out that this fivefold division represents the meticulous structural design of the Supreme Lord. The individual's sensory and physical systems are meant to operate in complete harmony with the external cosmos, both working as functional parts within the vast cosmic body of Brahman.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya explains that the groups of five are overseen by specific cosmic administrators (Devatas), who in turn are entirely dependent on five specific dynamic aspects of the Supreme Lord Vishnu. The meditation reminds the soul that it cannot move, breathe, or perceive without divine orchestration.

verses.sec: Śīkṣāvallī — Phonetics and Disciplines (Chapter I)
verses.dev: ओमिति ब्रह्म । ओमीतीदँ सर्वम् । ओमित्येतदनुकृति ह स्म वा अप्यो श्रावयेत्याश्रावयन्ति । ओमिति सामानि गायन्ति । ओँ शोमिति शस्त्राणि शँसन्ति । ओमित्यध्वर्युः प्रतिगरं प्रतिगृणाति । ओमिति ब्रह्मा प्रसौति । ओमित्यग्निहोत्रमनुजानाति । ओमिति ब्राह्मणः प्रवक्ष्यन्नाहा ब्रह्मोपाप्नवानीति । ब्रह्मैवोपाप्नोति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: ōmiti brahma | ōmītidã sarvam | ōmityētadanukṛti ha sma vā apyō śrāvayētyāśrāvayanti | ōmiti sāmāni gāyanthi | õ śōmiti śastrāṇi śãsanthi | ōmityadhvaryuḥ pratigaraṃ pratigṛṇāti | ōmiti brahmā prasauti | ōmityagnihōtramanujānāti | ōmiti brāhmaṇaḥ pravakṣyannāhā brahmōpāpnavānīti | brahmaivōpāpnōti || 1 ||
verses.trans: OM is Brahman. OM is all this. OM is used to signify compliance; when the priests say 'O Shravaya,' they clear the path for the mantra. With OM, they sing the Sama hymns. With OM Shom, they recite the Shastras. With OM, the Adhvaryu priest utters the response. With OM, the Brahma priest gives assent. With OM, the priest permits the fire sacrifice. With OM, a Brahmin begins to lecture, praying 'May I attain Brahman,' and he indeed attains Brahman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara labels OM as the ultimate symbol (Pratīka) and vehicle for both Saguṇa (conditioned) and Nirguṇa (unconditioned) Brahman. Since sounds comprise words, and words map out the universe, the sound OM (the source of all articulation) represents the whole universe. Meditating on OM acts as the highest mental shortcut to non-dual absorption.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The tradition highlights OM as the definitive acoustic name of Narayana. Its liturgical use across every Vedic ritual shows that no act of sacrifice or charity yields an eternal fruit unless it is bound to the supreme name of God.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva asserts that OM consists of three syllables (A, U, M) denoting the three major attributes of Vishnu: creation, maintenance, and dissolution. It is the absolute key to spiritual study; pronouncing it with devotion connects the finite mind directly to the infinite source.

verses.sec: Śīkṣāvallī — Phonetics and Disciplines (Chapter I)
verses.dev: ऋतं च स्वाध्यायप्रवचने च । सत्यं च स्वाध्यायप्रवचने च । तपश्च स्वाध्यायप्रवचने च । दमश्च स्वाध्यायप्रवचने च । शमश्च स्वाध्यायप्रवचने च । अग्नयश्च स्वाध्यायप्रवचने च । अग्निहोत्रं च स्वाध्यायप्रवचने च । अतिथयश्च स्वाध्यायप्रवचने च । मानुषं च स्वाध्यायप्रवचने च । प्रजा च स्वाध्यायप्रवचने च । प्रजनश्च स्वाध्यायप्रवचने च । प्रजातिश्च स्वाध्यायप्रवचने च । सत्यमिति सत्यवचा राथीतरः । तप इति तपोनित्यः पौरुशिष्टिः । स्वाध्यायप्रवचने एवेति नाको मौद्गल्यः । तद्धि तपस्तद्धि तपः ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: ṛtaṃ ca svādhyāyapravacane ca | satyaṃ ca svādhyāyapravacane ca | tapaśca svādhyāyapravacane ca | damaśca svādhyāyapravacane ca | śamaśca svādhyāyapravacane ca | agnayaśca svādhyāyapravacane ca | agnihotraṃ ca svādhyāyapravacane ca | atithayaśca svādhyāyapravacane ca | mānuṣaṃ ca svādhyāyapravacane ca | prajā ca svādhyāyapravacane ca | prajanaśca svādhyāyapravacane ca | prajātiśca svādhyāyapravacane ca | satyamiti satyavacā rāthītaraḥ | tapa iti taponityaḥ pauruśiṣṭiḥ | svādhyāyapravacane eveti nāko maudgalyaḥ | taddhi tapastaddhi tapaḥ || 1 ||
verses.trans: Righteousness along with personal study and teaching; Truth along with study and teaching; Austerity (Tapas) with study and teaching; Self-control (Dama) with study and teaching; Tranquility (Shama) with study and teaching; Maintenance of sacrificial fires with study and teaching; Performance of fire-sacrifices with study and teaching; Honoring guests with study and teaching; Humane duties with study and teaching; Procreation, marital duties, and raising a family along with study and teaching. Rathitara says 'Truth alone is essential'; Purusisti says 'Austerity alone is essential'; Maudgalya says 'Study and teaching are supreme.' That indeed is the highest penance.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara points out that the text deliberately repeats 'Svādhyāya-Pravacane' (personal study and teaching) after every single virtue. This underscores that ethical virtues are not static values; they must be kept alive, updated, and transmitted through generation after generation of active scholars.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights the value placed on family life and social duties (Prajati, Atithi). This proves that ancient spirituality did not require early escapism; rather, fulfilling social and biological duties with an attitude of worship is a legitimate, steady path to God.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that 'Tapas' and 'Satyam' are forms of internal mental discipline dedicated to Vishnu. The different views of the Rishis are not contradictory but highlight distinct components of a balanced, pious life centered around understanding scriptural truths.

verses.sec: Śīkṣāvallī — Phonetics and Disciplines (Chapter I)
verses.dev: अहं वृक्षस्य रेरिवा । कीर्तिः पृष्ठं गिरेरिव । ऊर्ध्वपवित्रो वाजिनीव स्वमृतमस्मि । द्रविणं सवर्चसम् । सुमेधा अमृतोक्षितः । इति त्रिशङ्कोर्वेदानुवचनम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: ahaṃ vṛkṣasya rerivā | kīrtiḥ pṛṣṭhaṃ gireriva | ūrdhvapavitro vājinīva svamṛtamasmi | draviṇaṃ savarcasam | sumedhā amṛtokṣitaḥ | iti triśaṅkorvedānuvacanam || 1 ||
verses.trans: I am the mover of the tree of the universe. My fame is radiant like the peak of a mountain. My source is pure, immortal, and luminous, like the nectar in the sun. I am the wealth of brilliant spiritual light, endowed with a clear intellect, immortal and undecaying. This is Trisanku’s self-declaration after attaining the wisdom of the Veda.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: A classic proclamation of ultimate self-realization (Jivanmukti). Shankara shows that Trisanku, through non-dual insight, experienced his absolute identity with Brahman. He is the 'mover of the tree of transience' (Samsara-vriksha), looking down upon the worldly sphere from the absolute peak of pure unconditioned Awareness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The tradition interprets this as an expression of a liberated soul (*Mukta*) who, by deep contemplation on the inner controller, feels completely integrated with the divine energy, reflecting the majestic qualities of God.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva clarifies that Trisanku is singing praises of the Supreme Lord residing within him. The 'tree' is the material world sustained by Vishnu; Trisanku acknowledges that the ultimate power driving his existence and glory belongs entirely to the Lord.

verses.sec: Śīkṣāvallī — Phonetics and Disciplines (Chapter I)
verses.dev: वेदमनूच्याचार्योऽन्तेवासिनमनुशास्ति । सत्यं वद । धर्मं चर । स्वाध्यायान्मा प्रमदः । आचार्याय प्रियं धनमाहृत्य प्रजातन्तुं मा व्यवच्छेत्सीः । सत्यान्न प्रमदितव्यम् । धर्मान्न प्रमदितव्यम् । कुशलान्न प्रमदितव्यम् । भूत्यै न प्रमदितव्यम् । स्वाध्यायप्रवचनाभ्यां न प्रमदितव्यम् । देवपितृकार्याभ्यां न प्रमदितव्यम् । मातृदेवो भव । पितृदेवो भव । आचार्यदेवो भव । अतिथिदेवो भव । यान्यनवद्यानि कर्माणि । तानि सेवितव्यानि । नो इतराणि । ... ॥ १-४ ॥
verses.iast: vedamanūcyācāryo'ntevāsinamanuśāsti | satyaṃ vada | dharmaṃ cara | svādhyāyānmā pramadaḥ | ācāryāya priyaṃ dhanamāhṛtya prajātantuṃ mā vyavacchetsīḥ | satyānna pramaditavyam | dharmānna pramaditavyam | kuśalānna pramaditavyam | bhūtyai n pramaditavyam | svādhyāyapravacanābhyāṃ na pramaditavyam | devapitṛkāryābhāṃ na pramaditavyam | mātṛdevo bhava | pitṛdevo bhava | ācāryadevo bhava | atithidevo bhava | yānyanavadyāni karmāṇi | tāni sevitavyāni | no itarāṇi | ... || 1-4 ||
verses.trans: Having taught the Veda, the teacher instructs the departing student: Speak the Truth. Practice Righteousness. Do not neglect your personal study. Having brought the teacher the wealth he desires, do not break the lineage of children. Do not swerve from Truth. Do not swerve from Dharma. Do not neglect your health and well-being. Do not neglect prosperity. Do not neglect study and teaching. Do not neglect your duties to the gods and ancestors. Treat your mother as a god. Treat your father as a god. Treat your teacher as a god. Treat your guest as a god. Whatever actions are blameless, those should be practiced, none else.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara emphasizes that this text shows that ethical and ritual duties (Karma) are compulsory for maintaining social order and purifying the mind. However, they are preliminary; their purpose is to qualify the student for the subsequent absolute renunciation (*Sannyasa*) introduced in the next chapters.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The command to respect elders, parents, and teachers as manifestations of God underscores the concept that the Supreme Lord is immediately accessible through loving service within standard human relationships.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva highlights that 'Truth' and 'Dharma' are fixed, unchanging commands of the Lord. The teacher’s final words instruct the student on how to navigate the material world without losing their moral compass and devotional anchor.

verses.sec: Śīkṣāvallī — Phonetics and Disciplines (Chapter I)
verses.dev: शं नो मित्रः शं वरुणः । शं नो भवत्यर्यमा । शं न इन्द्रो बृहस्पतिः । शं नो विष्णुरुरुक्रमः । नमो ब्रह्मणे । नमस्ते वायो । त्वमेव प्रत्यक्षं ब्रह्मासि । त्वामेव प्रत्यक्षं ब्रह्मावादिषम् । ऋतमवादिषम् । सत्यमवादिषम् । तन्मामावीत् । तद्वक्तारमावीत् । आवीन्माम् । आवीद्वक्तारम् । ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: śaṃ no mitraḥ śaṃ varuṇaḥ | śaṃ no bhavatyaryamā | śaṃ na indro bṛhaspatiḥ | śaṃ no viṣṇururukramaḥ | namo brahmaṇe | namaste vāyo | tvameva pratyakṣaṃ brahmāsi | tvāmeva pratyakṣaṃ brahmā vādiṣam | ṛtamavādiṣam | satyamavādiṣam | tanmāmāvīt | tadvaktāramāvīt | āvīnmām | āvīdvaktāram | oṃ śāntiḥ śāntiḥ śāntiḥ || 1 ||
verses.trans: May Mitra be propitious to us, and Varuna be propitious to us. May Aryaman be propitious to us. May Indra and Brihaspati be propitious to us. May Vishnu of wide strides be propitious to us. Salutation to Brahman. Salutation to thee, O Vayu. Thou indeed art the perceptible Brahman. Thee alone I have declared as the perceptible Brahman. I have spoken of the Right. I have spoken of the Truth. That has protected me. That has protected the teacher. It has protected me, it has protected the teacher. Om, Peace, Peace, Peace.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara points out a subtle grammatical change: while Anuvaka 1 used the future tense ('I *shall* speak of truth'), this closing prayer uses the past tense ('I *have* spoken of truth'). The student acknowledges that the preparatory phase of code, conduct, and grammar has been successfully fulfilled.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The prayer expresses gratitude to the internal guide for leading both master and student safely through the foundational stage of text study without internal divisions or friction.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reiterates that Vayu (Mukhya-Prana) has carried out his duty perfectly, enabling the master to teach and the student to absorb the cosmic truths correctly, paving the path for final realization.

verses.sec: Brahmānandavallī — The Bliss of Brahman (Chapter II)
verses.dev: ॐ ब्रह्मविदाप्नोति परम् । तदेषाभ्युक्ता । सत्यं ज्ञानमनन्तं ब्रह्म । यो वेद निहितं गुहायां परमे व्योमन् । सोऽश्नुते सर्वान्कामान्सह । ब्रह्मणा विपश्चितेति ॥ तस्माद्वा एतस्मादात्मन आकाशः सम्भूतः । आकाशाद्वायुः । वायोरग्निः । अग्नेरापः । अद्भ्यः पृथिवी । पृथिव्या ओषधयः । ओषधीभ्योऽन्नम् । अन्नात्पुरुषः । स वा एष पुरुषोऽन्नरसमयः । ... ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: oṃ brahmavidāpnoti param | tadeṣābhyuktā | satyaṃ jñānamanantaṃ brahma | yo veda nihitaṃ guhāyāṃ parame vyoman | so'śnute sarvānkāmānsaha | brahmaṇā vipaściteti || tasmādvā etasmādātmana ākāśaḥ sambhūtaḥ | ākāśādvāyuḥ | vāyoragniḥ | agnerāpaḥ | adbhyaḥ pṛthivī | pṛthivyā oṣadhayaḥ | oṣadhībhyo'nnam | annātpuruṣaḥ | sa vā eṣa puruṣo'nnarasamayaḥ | ... || 1 ||
verses.trans: Om. The knower of Brahman attains the Supreme. With reference to that, this is chanted: 'Brahman is Truth, Knowledge, and Infinity. He who knows It hidden in the cave of the heart, in the highest space, enjoys all desires simultaneously with the omniscient Brahman.' From that Self, indeed, space was born; from space, air; from air, fire; from fire, water; from water, earth; from earth, herbs; from herbs, food; and from food, man. This man, indeed, consists of the essence of food.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara's commentary here is monumental. He defines 'Satya' as that which never changes its nature, 'Jñāna' as pure awareness (not a mental act or a knowing subject), and 'Ananta' as free from spatial, temporal, or objective limitations. Brahman is not *endowed* with truth or knowledge; Brahman *is* existence-consciousness itself. The 'cave' (Guhā) is the intellect; realizing Brahman there dissolves the illusion of a separate ego, fulfilling all desires instantly because the realized sage is identical with the whole cosmos.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja argues that 'Satya', 'Jñāna', and 'Ananta' are distinct auspicious attributes (*kalyāṇa-guṇas*) that uniquely qualify the Supreme Personality (Parabrahman), distinguishing Him from insentient matter (*Acit*) and bound/freed souls (*Cit*). The progressive creation from space down to the human body shows how the universe is an evolutionary expression of the divine will, with the Lord remaining the inner soul (*Antaryāmin*) of every stage.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya rejects any absolute identity between the knower and Brahman. 'Attaining Brahman' means a liberated soul enters into a state of close communion and experiences bliss dependent on and granted by the grace of God. The terms Truth, Knowledge, and Infinity assert that the Lord is the absolute independent source of all reality, completely distinct from the dependent cosmic elements like space, air, and the physical bodies of living beings.

verses.sec: Brahmānandavallī — The Bliss of Brahman (Chapter II)
verses.dev: अन्नाद्वै प्रजाः प्रजायन्ते । याः काश्च पृथिवीं श्रिताः । अथो अन्नेनैव जीवन्ति । अथैनदपि यन्त्यन्ततः । ... तस्माद्वा एतस्मादन्नरसमयात् । अन्योऽन्तर आत्मा प्राणमयः । तेनैष पूर्णः । स वा एष पुरुषविध एव । ... ॥ १-२ ॥
verses.iast: annādvai prajāḥ prajāyante | yāḥ kāśca pṛthivīṃ śritāḥ | atho annenaiva jīvanti | athainadapi yantyantataḥ | ... tasmādvā etasmādannarasamayāt | anyo'ntara ātmā prāṇamayaḥ | tenaiṣa pūrṇaḥ | sa vā eṣa puruṣavidha eva | ... || 1-2 ||
verses.trans: From food, indeed, all creatures are produced—whatever beings dwell on earth. By food, they live, and into it they finally return. ... Distinct from this body consisting of the essence of food, there is another internal self consisting of the vital force (Prāna). By that, this physical form is completely filled. This vital self is also shaped like a human being.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara notes that the text employs the method of *Arundhatī-darśana-nyāya* (leading from the gross to the subtle). The physical body (Annamaya) is the most external, superficial layer of identity. By looking inside, the student discovers the Prāṇamaya self—the physiological system of breath and bio-energy. It takes the shape of the physical body just as melted copper takes the shape of the crucibles into which it is poured.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Prāṇamaya sheath is interpreted as the complex life-support network engineered by the Divine to sustain the physical instrument. It is called an 'inner self' relative to the physical shell, but it is still fundamentally material, serving as a layer through which the indwelling soul manifests its physical actions.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the Prāṇamaya layer is controlled by the subordinate deity Vāyu. The vital breath animates the physical meat-suit under the precise command of the Lord within, showing that the physical body is completely dependent on subtle life forces to operate.

verses.sec: Brahmānandavallī — The Bliss of Brahman (Chapter II)
verses.dev: प्राणं देवा अनुप्राणन्ति । मनुष्याः पशवश्च ये । प्राणो हि भूतानामायुः । तस्मात्सर्वआयुषमुच्यते । ... तस्माद्वा एतस्मात्प्राणमयात् । अन्योऽन्तर आत्मा मनोमयः । तेनैष पूर्णः । स वा एष पुरुषविध एव । ... ॥ १-२ ॥
verses.iast: prāṇaṃ devā anuprāṇanti | manuṣyāḥ paśavaśca ye | prāṇo hi bhūtānāmāyuḥ | tasmātsarvaāyuṣamucyate | ... tasmādvā etasmātprāṇamayāt | anyo'ntara ātmā manomayaḥ | tenaiṣa pūrṇaḥ | sa vā eṣa puruṣavidha eva | ... || 1-2 ||
verses.trans: The gods breathe after the vital force, as do humans and animals. Prāna is indeed the life-span of all beings; therefore it is called the universal life-span. ... Distinct from this vital self, there is another internal self consisting of Mind (Manas). By that, the vital self is completely filled. This mental self is also shaped like a human being.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that 'Mind' here means the continuous stream of thoughts, desires, doubts, and emotional patterns. The text metaphorically constructs this sheath like a sacrificial bird, where the Yajur-veda is its head, the Rig-veda its right wing, and the Sama-veda its left wing. This means that even regular religious thoughts and scriptural rituals belong strictly to the mind-sheath, not to the ultimate unconditioned Consciousness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Manomaya sheath represents the cognitive aspect of the individual soul's empirical experience. The internal organs of perception process sensory details under this layer, enabling the living being to interface with external objects and perform Vedic sacrifices.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva highlights that the mind can either be a source of bondage or a means of release. It is populated by sensory desires, but when aligned with the scriptural commands (the Vedas acting as its metaphorical limbs), it becomes the instrument for developing pure devotion to Vishnu.

verses.sec: Brahmānandavallī — The Bliss of Brahman (Chapter II)
verses.dev: यतो वाचो निवर्तन्ते । अप्राप्य मनसा सह । आनन्दं ब्रह्मणो विद्वान । न बिभेति कदाचनेति । ... तस्माद्वा एतस्मान्मनमयात् । अन्योऽन्तर आत्मा विज्ञानमयः । तेनैष पूर्णः । स वा एष पुरुषविध एव । ... ॥ १-२ ॥
verses.iast: yato vāco nivartante | aprāpya manasā saha | ānandaṃ brahmaṇo vidvāna | na bibheti kadācaneti | ... tasmādvā etasmātmanomayāt | anyo'ntara ātmā vijñānamayaḥ | tenaiṣa pūrṇaḥ | sa vā eṣa puruṣavidha eva | ... || 1-2 ||
verses.trans: He who knows the bliss of Brahman, from which all words return along with the mind without reaching it, never fears anything. ... Distinct from this mental self, there is another internal self consisting of Intellect (Vijñāna). By that, the mental self is completely filled. This intellectual self is also shaped like a human being.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The famous verse 'Yato vāco nivartante' proves that absolute Brahman cannot be conceptualized by thoughts or designated by words. Moving past the fluctuating mind, the text reveals the Vijñānamaya self—the intellect that determines, decides, and holds the sense of agency ('I am the doer'). Faith (*Śraddhā*) is its head, Righteousness (*Ṛtam*) its right wing, and Truth (*Satyam*) its left wing. It is deeper than emotion, yet it is still a finite modification of human consciousness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The intellectual sheath is the seat of the individual soul's *Dharmabhūta-jñāna* (attributive consciousness) which enables deliberate judgment and spiritual discrimination. The soul under this sheath realizes itself as an agent capable of pursuing salvation through structured knowledge and meditation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets the intellectual sheath as the seat of direct mental clarity and localized understanding. It is distinct from the mind because it deals with concrete judgment and spiritual determination, guided directly by the indwelling wisdom of the Lord.

verses.sec: Brahmānandavallī — The Bliss of Brahman (Chapter II)
verses.dev: विज्ञानं यज्ञं तनुते । कर्माणि तनुतेऽपि च । विज्ञानं देवाः सर्वे । ब्रह्म ज्येष्ठमुपासते । ... तस्माद्वा एतस्माद्विज्ञानमयात् । अन्योऽन्तर आत्माऽऽनन्दमयः । तेनैष पूर्णः । स वा एष पुरुषविध एव । ... ॥ १-२ ॥
verses.iast: vijñānaṃ yajñaṃ tanute | karmāṇi tanute'pi ca | vijñānaṃ devāḥ sarve | brahma jyeṣṭhamupāsate | ... tasmādvā etasmādvijñānamayāt | anyo'ntara ātmā''nandamayah | tenaiṣa pūrṇaḥ | sa vā eṣa puruṣavidha eva | ... || 1-2 ||
verses.trans: Intellect performs the sacrifice, and it also performs all duties. All the gods worship the intellect as Brahman, the eldest. ... Distinct from this intellectual self, there is another internal self consisting of Bliss (Ānanda). By that, the intellectual self is completely filled. This blissful self is also shaped like a human being.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: A critical battlefield for commentators. Shankara insists that *Ānandamaya* is still a *kosha* (a sheath or covering), meaning it is a product of ignorance (*Avidyā*). It represents the state of deep sleep or the temporary joy experienced when a desired object is attained. Joy (*Priya*) is its head, delight (*Moda*) its right wing, and great delight (*Pramoda*) its left wing. But the real Brahman is not the Anandamaya itself; rather, Brahman is the foundational support (*Brahma puccham pratiṣṭhā*) upon which this final sheath rests.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school fiercely counters Shankara. Rāmānuja argues in his *Śrī Bhāṣya* that 'Anandamaya' refers directly to the Supreme Lord Himself, who possesses infinite, unconditioned bliss. The suffix *maya* here indicates abundance (*prācurya*), not modification or illusion. The Supreme Soul is the very embodiment of bliss, residing deep within the heart of the individual soul as its final inward destination.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva also holds that Anandamaya is the Supreme Lord Vishnu, completely transcending the finite, material sheaths. The individual soul's innate bliss is tiny and dependent, whereas the Anandamaya Purusha is the infinite ocean of joy that supports all creation. The 'limbs' and 'wings' described are the various blissful forms and absolute expressions of the divine personality.

verses.sec: Brahmānandavallī — The Bliss of Brahman (Chapter II)
verses.dev: असन्नेव स भवति । असद्ब्रह्मेति वेद चेत् । अस्ति ब्रह्मेति चेद्वेद । सन्तमेनं ततो विदुरिति । तस्यैष एव शारीर आत्मा । यः पूर्वस्य । अथातोऽनुप्रश्नाः । उताविद्वानमुं लोकं प्रेत्य । कश्चन गच्छती ३ । आहो विद्वानमुं लोकं प्रेत्य । कश्चित्समश्नुता ३ उ । सोऽकामयत । बहु स्यां प्रजायेयेति । स तपोऽतप्यत । स तपस्तप्त्वा । इदँ सर्वमसृजत । यदिदं किञ्च । तत्सृष्ट्वा । तदेवानुप्राविशत् । ... ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: asanneva sa bhavati | asadbrahmeti veda cet | asti brahmeti ceadveda | santamenaṃ tato viduriti | tasyaiṣa eva śārīra ātmā | yaḥ pūrvasya | athāto'nupraśnāḥ | utāvidvānamuṃ lokaṃ pretya | kaścana gacchatī 3 | āho vidvānamuṃ lokaṃ pretya | kaścitsamaśnutā 3 u | so'kāmayata | bahu syāṃ prajāyeyeti | sa tapo'tpyata | sa tapastaptvā | idã sarvamasṛjata | yadidaṃ kiñca | tatsṛṣṭvā | tade vānupraviśat | ... || 1 ||
verses.trans: If a person knows Brahman as non-existent, he himself becomes non-existent. If he knows that Brahman exists, then the wise know him as truly existing. ... He (the Supreme Self) desired: 'May I become many, may I grow forth.' He performed austerity. Having performed austerity, He projected all this—whatever there is. Having projected it, He entered into it.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara clarifies that a person who denies Brahman essentially denies his own reality, since Brahman is the underlying consciousness of the denier. The cosmic 'desire' and 'austerity' are not human emotional acts; they signify the spontaneous activation of Maya, projecting the names and forms of the empirical universe. The 'entry' (*Anupraveśa*) of Brahman into creation is a metaphorical way of saying that pure consciousness is now seemingly localized and reflected within the intellect of living beings, like the sun reflecting in water vessels.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja points out that before creation, names and forms were in a subtle, unmanifested state (*Sūkṣma-Cidacit*). The Lord's desire to 'become many' is the dynamic transition into the gross, manifested state (*Sthūla-Cidacit*). The universe is not an illusion; it is a real, physical modification of His body. His 'entry' means He accompanies the individual souls as their constant inner guide, dwelling within even the inanimate matter.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya strongly asserts that the Lord does not physically transform Himself into the universe, nor does He undergo change. The creation of the 'many' indicates His independent power to bring forth completely distinct entities into active manifestation. His 'entry' denotes His complete pervasiveness—the omnipresent, active placement of His divine control within every atom of space to sustain its existence.

verses.sec: Brahmānandavallī — The Bliss of Brahman (Chapter II)
verses.dev: असद्वा इदमग्र आसीत् । ततो वै सदजायत । तदात्मानँ स्वयमकुरुत । तस्मात्तत्सुकृतमुच्यत इति । यद्वै तत्सुकृतम् । रसो वै सः । रँ ह्येवायं लब्ध्वाऽऽनन्दी भवति । को ह्येवान्यात्कः प्राण्यात् । यदेष आकाश आनन्दो न स्यात् । एष ह्येवाऽऽनन्दयाति । यदा ह्येवैष एतस्मिन्मदृश्येऽनात्म्येऽनिरुक्तेऽनिलयनेऽभयं प्रतिष्ठां विन्दते । अथ सोऽभयं गतो भवति । ... ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: asadvā idamagra āsīt | tato vai sadajāyata | tadātmānã svayamakuruta | tasmāttatsukṛtamucyata iti | yadvai tatsukṛtam | raso vai saḥ | rã hyevāyaṃ labdhvā''nandī bhavati | ko hyevānyātkaḥ prāṇyāt | yadeṣa ākāśa ānando na syāt | eṣa hyevā''nandayāti | yadā hyevaiṣa etasminnadṛśye'nātmye'nirukte'nilayane'bhayaṃ pratiṣṭhāṃ vindate | atha so'bhayaṃ gato bhavati | ... || 1 ||
verses.trans: In the beginning, all this was indeed unmanifested (Asat). From that, the manifested (Sat) was born. That Self created Itself by Itself. Therefore, It is called self-created. That which is self-created is verily the essence of delight (Rasa). For truly, on perceiving this essence, one becomes full of bliss. Who indeed could breathe, who could live, if this blissful space did not exist? He alone bestows bliss. When a seeker finds fearless support in this invisible, unembodied, indefinable, and abodeless Absolute, then he expands completely into fearlessness.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that 'Asat' here does not mean absolute nothingness, but rather the unmanifested state of the universe prior to names and forms. Brahman is 'Sukritam' because It is the unique, ultimate cause that requires no outside agent to shape It. Brahman is 'Rasa'—the self-luminous joy behind all empirical experiences. Fear arises only when there is an absolute perception of duality or a sense of separation (*Udaramantaram*). The moment non-dual realization breaks the illusion of otherness, fear vanishes permanently.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school views 'Asat' as the primordial condition where matter and souls are so subtle that they practically lack distinct definitions. The transformation into 'Sat' is the Lord structuring this material into a distinct, living universe. He is called 'Rasa' because He is the sweet, personal object of supreme devotion. Fearing nothing occurs when the individual soul surrenders completely to the protection of the Lord, recognizing that nothing exists outside His cosmic sovereignty.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva glosses 'Asat' as that which is different from the fully visible world. The Lord is 'Sukritam' because His actions are perfectly executed, pristine, and independent. He is the independent 'Rasa' (joy), and all individual living souls breathe and live entirely because He pumps life-force and happiness into their finite frameworks. Realizing the total majesty and absolute difference of the Lord gives the soul safe sanctuary under His ultimate rule.

verses.sec: Brahmānandavallī — The Bliss of Brahman (Chapter II)
verses.dev: भीषास्माद्वातः पवते । भीषोदेति सूर्यः । भीषास्मादग्निश्चेन्द्रश्च । मृत्युर्धावति पञ्चम इति । सैषानन्दस्य मीमाँसा भवति । युवा स्यात्साधुयुवाऽध्यायकः । आशिष्ठो द्रढिष्ठो बलिष्ठः । तस्येयं पृथिवी सर्वा वित्तस्य पूर्णा स्यात् । स एको मानुष आनन्दः । ते ये शतं मानुषा आनन्दाः । ... स यश्चायं पुरुषे । यश्चासावादित्ये । स एकः । ... ॥ १-५ ॥
verses.iast: bhīṣāsmādvātaḥ pavate | bhīṣodeti sūryaḥ | bhīṣāsmādagniścentraśca | mṛtyurdhāvati pañcama iti | saiṣānandasya mīmā̃sā bhavati | yuvā syātsādhuyuvā'dhyāyakaḥ | āśiṣṭho draḍhiṣṭho baliṣṭhaḥ | tasyeyaṃ pṛthivī sarvā vittasya pūrṇā syāt | sa eko mānuṣa ānandaḥ | te ye śataṃ mānuṣā ānandāḥ | ... sa yaścāyaṃ puruṣe | yaścāsā vāditye | sa ekaḥ | ... || 1-5 ||
verses.trans: Out of fear of Him, the wind blows. Out of fear, the sun rises. Out of fear, fire, Indra, and Death, the fifth, speed along their tracks. This is the scientific inquiry into Bliss: Let there be a noble young man, well-read, highly disciplined, resolute, and physically strong, and let the entire earth be full of wealth for him. This is one unit of human bliss. A hundredfold of this human bliss equals one unit of bliss of the human Gandharvas... [the scale ascends 100x through celestial realms up to Prajapati and Brahman]... He who is here in man, and He who is there in the sun, are one and the same.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara notes that the elemental forces function systematically because they are bound by the unyielding cosmic laws of Brahman. The 'Calculus of Bliss' mathematically proves that worldly pleasure is a tiny, conditional fraction of absolute joy. Each higher realm multiplies happiness by a factor of 100, but the text adds after each stage: 'this same bliss belongs to the sage who is free from desires (*Akāmahata*)'. This demonstrates that a person does not need to travel to heaven to find happiness; by eliminating egoic desire, the non-dual sage enjoys the absolute bliss of Brahman right here and now.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The cosmic controllers move out of deep reverence and awe for the Supreme Command of Ishvara. The ascending scale of bliss measures the increasing capacity of souls in different structural environments to experience the joy of the Divine. The ultimate conclusion—that the inner soul of man is identical to the inner soul of the sun—confirms that the organic thread connecting all components of creation is the singular, glorious presence of the Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets the 'fear' as a voluntary, disciplined compliance by the devas to the cosmic laws set by Vishnu. He emphasizes that the graded hierarchy of bliss (*Ānanda-Tāratamya*) is real and persists even in the state of final liberation (*Mukti*). Souls have different fixed capacities for bliss based on their innate nature, but all are filled to their perfect comfort level by the grace of the Supreme Lord, who is the absolute, uncompromised fountainhead of infinite joy.

verses.sec: Brahmānandavallī — The Bliss of Brahman (Chapter II)
verses.dev: यतो वाचो निवर्तन्ते । अप्राप्य मनसा saha । आनन्दं ब्रह्मणो विद्वान् । न बिभेति कुतश्चनेति । एतँ ह वाव न तपति । किमहँ साधु नाकरवम् । किमहं पापमकरवमिति । स य एवं विद्वानेते आत्मानँ स्पृणुते । उभे ह्येवैष एते आत्मानँ स्पृणुते । य एवं वेद । इत्युपनिषत् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: yato vāco nivartante | aprāpya manasā saha | ānandaṃ brahmaṇo vidvān | na bibheti kutaścāneti | etã ha vāva na tapati | kimahã sādhu nākaravam | kimahaṃ pāpamakaravamiti | sa ya evaṃ vidvānete ātmānã spṛṇute | ubhe hyevaiṣa ete ātmānã spृṇute | ya evaṃ veda | ityupaniṣat || 1 ||
verses.trans: He who knows the bliss of Brahman, from which words recede along with the mind without attaining it, fears nothing whatsoever. Such a realized soul is never tormented by thoughts like: 'Why did I not do what is good? Why did I commit what is evil?' He who knows this looks upon both of these as variations of the same Self. He cherishes both as manifestations of the Spirit. Thus ends the secret doctrine.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The perfect conclusion to the Brahmānandavallī. Shankara explains that conventional dualistic morality operates in the realm of the ego. The knower of Nirguṇa Brahman transcends the anxiety of karmic guilt ('good vs. evil') because he sees that actions, virtues, and sins are mere appearances on the screen of consciousness. Having dissolved the false doer-identity (*Kartṛtva*), he realizes he was never the actor, experiencing absolute, unchanging peace.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The soul who has realized its ultimate nature as a dependent mode of the Lord is freed from the crushing weight of existential guilt. Sins and omissions are washed away by loving self-surrender (*Prapatti*). The realized devotee sees both past good and bad karma absorbed and neutralized by the infinite grace and protective will of the Creator.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva clarifies that this does not encourage ethical lawlessness. Rather, the liberated soul becomes so completely aligned with the divine will that it naturally ceases to commit sin or feel worldly anxiety. It understands that all moral and physical consequences are managed by the righteous governance of Vishnu, enabling the soul to rest in pure, unadulterated devotional ecstasy.

verses.sec: Bhṛguvallī — The Inquiry of Bhṛgu (Chapter III)
verses.dev: भृगुर्वै वारुणिः । वरुणं पितरमुपससार । अधीहि भगवो ब्रह्मेति । तस्मा एतत्प्रोवाच । अन्नं प्राणं चक्षुः श्रोत्रं मनो वाचमिति । तँ होवाच । यतो वा इमानि भूतानि जायन्ते । येन जातानि जीवन्ति । यत्प्रयन्त्यभिसंविशन्ति । तद्विजिज्ञासस्व । तद्ब्रह्मेति । स तपोऽतप्यत । स तपस्तप्त्वा ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: bhṛgurvai vāruṇiḥ | varuṇaṃ pitaramupasasāra | adhīhi bhagavo brahmeti | tasmā etatprovāca | annaṃ prāṇaṃ cakṣuḥ śrotraṃ mano vācamiti | tã hōvāca | yato vā imāni bhūtāni jāyante | yena jātāni jīvanti | yatprayantyabhisaṃviśanti | tadvijijñāsasva | tadbrahmeti | sa tapō'tapyata | sa tapastaptvā || 1 ||
verses.trans: Bhṛgu, the son of Varuṇa, approached his father Varuṇa, saying: 'Revered Sir, teach me Brahman.' Varuṇa said to him: 'Food, vital force, eye, ear, mind, and speech are the gates to Brahman.' He further told him: 'That from which all these living beings are born, That by which they live after being born, and That into which they merge when departing—seek to know That. That is Brahman.' Bhṛgu performed austerity. Having performed austerity...

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara states that Varuṇa deliberately does not give a direct description; instead, he provides a structural definition (*Taṭastha-lakṣaṇa*) that outlines Brahman as the ultimate cause of cosmic creation, preservation, and dissolution. The 'austerity' (*Tapas*) practiced by Bhṛgu is not physical mortification, but the highly focused internal concentration of the mind and senses (*एकाग्रता*), which is the supreme means for realizing non-dual Truth.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja points out that the definition of Brahman given here corresponds precisely to the second aphorism of the Brahma Sūtras (*Janmādyasya yataḥ*). This proves that Brahman is the real, independent, and efficient cosmic cause of a real creation. The instruments listed (food, prāṇa, senses) are recognized as divine mechanisms meant to lead the soul toward its Master.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya highlights this verse to validate the absolute difference between God and the universe. Brahman alone is the complete sovereign creator, sustainer, and absorber. The fact that Bhṛgu must practice intense inquiry confirms that the individual soul lacks this omniscience and must actively seek the grace of the Supreme Lord to know Him.

verses.sec: Bhṛguvallī — The Inquiry of Bhṛgu (Chapter III)
verses.dev: अन्नं ब्रह्मेति व्यजानात । अन्नाद्ध्येव खल्विमानि भूतानि जायन्ते । अन्नेन जातानि जीवन्ति । अन्नं प्रयन्त्यभिसंविशन्तीति । तद्विज्ञाय । पुनरेव वरुणं पितरमुपससार । अधीहि भगवो ब्रह्मेति । तँ होवाच । तपसा ब्रह्म विजिज्ञासस्व । तपो ब्रह्मेति । स तपोऽतप्यत । स तपस्तप्त्वा ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: annaṃ brahmeti vyajānāt | annāddhyēva khalvimāni bhūtāni jāyante | annena jātāni jīvanti | annaṃ prayantyabhisaṃviśantīti | tadvijñāya | punareva varuṇaṃ pitaramupasasāra | adhīhi bhagavo brahmeti | tã hōvāca | tapasā brahma vijijñāsasva | tapō brahmeti | sa tapō'tapyata | sa tapastaptvā || 1 ||
verses.trans: Bhṛgu realized that food (matter) is Brahman. For truly, from food all these living beings are born; by food they live after being born; and into food they enter when departing. Having realized this, he approached his father Varuṇa again, saying: 'Revered Sir, teach me Brahman.' Varuṇa said to him: 'Seek to know Brahman through austerity. Austerity is Brahman.' He performed austerity. Having performed austerity...

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara comments that Bhṛgu initially stops at the grossest layer—materialist reductionism. While food fits the structural definition on a superficial physical level, Bhṛgu notices that matter is insentient, structural, and subject to decay. It cannot be the ultimate changeless substratum. His father sends him back to imply: 'Keep drilling deeper using your focused intellect.'
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The material world is a real form of the Lord, but identifying it as the absolute Brahman is incomplete. Bhṛgu must look beyond the external physical manifestation to discover the vital, internal governing principles that give matter its dynamic life.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that food is an objective expression of the Lord’s capacity to sustain physical entities. However, because it is dependent and non-intelligent, it cannot be the Supreme Lord. Bhṛgu is directed to continue his meditation to locate the higher controlling consciousness.

verses.sec: Bhṛguvallī — The Inquiry of Bhṛgu (Chapter III)
verses.dev: प्राणो ब्रह्मेति व्यजानात । प्राणाद्ध्येव खल्विमानि भूतानि जायन्ते । प्राणेन जातानि जीवन्ति । प्राणं प्रयन्त्यभिसंविशन्तीति । तद्विज्ञाय । पुनरेव वरुणं पितरमुपससार । अधीहि भगवो ब्रह्मेति । तँ होवाच । तपसा ब्रह्म विजिज्ञासस्व । तपो ब्रह्मेति । स तपोऽतप्यत । स तपस्तप्त्वा ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: prāṇo brahmeti vyajānāt | prāṇāddhyēva khalvimāni bhūtāni jāyante | prāṇena jātāni jīvanti | prāṇaṃ prayantyabhisaṃviśantīti | tadvijñāya | punareva varuṇaṃ pitaramupasasāra | adhīhi bhagavo brahmeti | tã hōvāca | tapasā brahma vijijñāsasva | tapō brahmeti | sa tapō'tapyata | sa tapastaptvā || 1 ||
verses.trans: Bhṛgu realized that the vital force (Prāṇa) is Brahman. For truly, from the vital force all these living beings are born; by the vital force they live after being born; and into the vital force they enter when departing. Having realized this, he approached his father Varuṇa again, saying: 'Revered Sir, teach me Brahman.' Varuṇa said to him: 'Seek to know Brahman through austerity. Austerity is Brahman.' He performed austerity. Having performed austerity...

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara notes that Prāṇa is superior to matter because it animates it. Yet, dynamic lifeforce alone is non-reflective; it functions blindly without localized self-awareness. It acts as a combination of forces and thus requires an underlying intelligent coordinator to give it direction.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Prāṇa represents a higher vital state of Nature under the control of the Lord. Bhṛgu recognizes its power but realizes that energy cannot be the final destination; it requires a conscious, psychological center to register and process experience.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The vital force is governed by Mukhya-Prāṇa, the chief agent of Vishnu. While it drives the physical systems, it operates strictly under orders. Bhṛgu's continued inquiry shows that he is searching for the ultimate independent Director behind this vital energy.

verses.sec: Bhṛguvallī — The Inquiry of Bhṛgu (Chapter III)
verses.dev: मनो ब्रह्मेति व्यजानात । मनसो ह्येव खल्विमानि भूतानि जायन्ते । मनसा जातानि जीवन्ति । मनः प्रयन्त्यभिसंविशन्तीति । तद्विज्ञाय । पुनरेव वरुणं पितरमुपससार । अधीहि भगवो ब्रह्मेति । तँ होवाच । तपसा ब्रह्म विजिज्ञासस्व । तपो ब्रह्मेति । स तपोऽतप्यत । स तपस्तप्त्वा ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: mano brahmeti vyajānāt | manaso hyēva khalvimāni bhūtāni jāyante | manasā jātāni jīvanti | manaḥ prayantyabhisaṃviśantīti | tadvijñāya | punareva varuṇaṃ pitaramupasasāra | adhīhi bhagavo brahmeti | tã hōvāca | tapasā brahma vijijñāsasva | tapō brahmeti | sa tapō'tapyata | sa tapastaptvā || 1 ||
verses.trans: Bhṛgu realized that Mind (Manas) is Brahman. For truly, from Mind all these living beings are born; by Mind they live after being born; and into Mind they enter when departing. Having realized this, he approached his father Varuṇa again, saying: 'Revered Sir, teach me Brahman.' Varuṇa said to him: 'Seek to know Brahman through austerity. Austerity is Brahman.' He performed austerity. Having performed austerity...

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The mind represents the psychological universe. Bhṛgu notices that the mind is a theater of constant flux, characterized by doubt, emotion, and shifting impressions (*Sankalpa-Vikalpa*). A fluctuating entity cannot be the ultimate unchangeable foundation of reality.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Mind is the organ of emotional and sensory coordination. Though it is subtler than breath, its vulnerability to delusion, passion, and fragmentation indicates that it is an instrument of the soul, not the Supreme Soul itself.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The mind is a dependent internal organ. Madhva observes that because the mind undergoes sleep, distraction, and ignorance, it cannot be identified with the omniscient Lord. Bhṛgu is urged to press onward to uncover the stabilizing faculty of higher wisdom.

verses.sec: Bhṛguvallī — The Inquiry of Bhṛgu (Chapter III)
verses.dev: विज्ञानं ब्रह्मेति व्यजानात । विज्ञानाद्ध्येव खल्विमानि भूतानि जायन्ते । विज्ञानेन जातानि जीवन्ति । विज्ञानं प्रयन्त्यभिसंविशन्तीति । तद्विज्ञाय । पुनरेव वरुणं पितरमुपससार । अधीहि भगवो ब्रह्मेति । तँ होवाच । तपसा ब्रह्म विजिज्ञासस्व । तपो ब्रह्मेति । स तपोऽतप्यत । स तपस्तप्त्वा ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: vijñānaṃ brahmeti vyajānāt | vijñānāddhyēva khalvimāni bhūtāni jāyante | vijñānena jātāni jīvanti | विज्ञानं prayantyabhisaṃviśantīti | tadvijñāya | punareva varuṇaṃ pitaramupasasāra | adhīhi bhagavo brahmeti | tã hōvāca | tapasā brahma vijijñāsasva | tapō brahmeti | sa tapō'tapyata | sa tapastaptvā || 1 ||
verses.trans: Bhṛgu realized that Intellect (Vijñāna) is Brahman. For truly, from Intellect all these living beings are born; by Intellect they live after being born; and into Intellect they enter when departing. Having realized this, he approached his father Varuṇa again, saying: 'Revered Sir, teach me Brahman.' Varuṇa said to him: 'Seek to know Brahman through austerity. Austerity is Brahman.' He performed austerity. Having performed austerity...

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Intellect is the deterministic faculty of judgment and ego-agency. Bhṛgu realizes that even the intellect operates with a subject-object split—it knows things as separate items. It is a localized tool of cognition and still bears the subtle seed of individual limitation and doership (*Kartṛtva*).
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Vijñāna is the individual soul's conscious discriminative capacity. While it distinguishes the soul from physical matter, this intellectual layer is a conditional asset that points toward the absolute source of knowledge, prompting Bhṛgu to seek the final substratum.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The intellectual sheath is the seat of individual judgment. Madhva notes that human judgment is finite and subject to structural errors. It cannot be the self-sustained, independent Brahman, indicating that the ultimate object of devotion lies deeper still.

verses.sec: Bhṛguvallī — The Inquiry of Bhṛgu (Chapter III)
verses.dev: आनंदो ब्रह्मेति व्यजानात । आनन्दाद्ध्येव खल्विमानि भूतानि जायन्ते । आनन्देन जातानि जीवन्ति । आनन्दं प्रयन्त्यभिसंविशन्तीति । सैषा भार्गवी वारुणी विद्या । परमे व्योमन्प्रतिष्ठिता । स य एवं वेद प्रतितिष्ठति । अन्नवानन्नादो भवति । महान्भवति प्रजया पशुभिर्ब्रह्मवर्चसेन । महान्कीर्त्या ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: ānando brahmeti vyajānāt | ānandāddhyēva khalvimāni bhūtāni jāyante | ānandena jātāni jīvanti | ānandaṃ prayantyabhisaṃviśantīti | saiṣā bhārgavī vāruṇī vidyā | parame vyōmanpratiṣṭhitā | sa ya evaṃ veda pratitiṣṭhati | annavānannādo bhavati | mahānbhavati prajayā paśubhirbrahmavarcasena | mahānkīrtyā || 1 ||
verses.trans: Bhṛgu realized that Bliss (Ānanda) is Brahman. For truly, from Bliss all these living beings are born; by Bliss they live after being born; and into Bliss they enter when departing. This is the sacred wisdom discovered by Bhṛgu and taught by Varuṇa, established in the highest space of the heart. He who knows this becomes firmly established. He becomes the possessor of food and the eater of food. He becomes great in progeny, in cattle, and in the radiance of sacred knowledge, and great in fame.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The climax of Bhṛgu’s inquiry. Shankara explains that when Bhṛgu reaches Bliss, he stops because it perfectly fulfills the cosmic definition. This Bliss is not an emotional feeling, but the unconditioned, non-dual, self-luminous Reality behind existence. The world is born from this Bliss, sustained by it, and dissolves back into it. The sage who realizes this truth loses his individual ego and abides permanently as pure, undivided Existence-Consciousness-Bliss.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Bhṛgu reaches the inner core of reality: the Supreme Lord Himself, who is the ocean of infinite, unconditioned Bliss (*Ānandamaya*). Finding this truth means the soul discovers its eternal home, resting within the glorious, auspicious nature of the Divine Personal God who fills all creation with joy.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva asserts that this final realization uncovers the Supreme Lord Vishnu as the absolute independent source of infinite bliss. The soul does not become Brahman; instead, it becomes fully established (*Pratitiṣṭhati*) in its true, pure nature of dependent joy, forever experiencing the loving shelter and sublime majesty of the Master.

verses.sec: Bhṛguvallī — The Inquiry of Bhṛgu (Chapter III)
verses.dev: अन्नं न निन्द्यात् । तद्व्रतम् । प्राणो वा अन्नम् । शरीरमन्नादम् । प्राणे शरीरं प्रतिष्ठितम् । शरीरे प्राणः प्रतिष्ठितः । तदेतदन्नमन्ने प्रतिष्ठितम् । स य एतदन्नमन्ने प्रतिष्ठितं वेद प्रतितिष्ठति । ... ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: annaṃ na nindyāt | tadvratam | prāṇo vā annam | śarīramannādam | prāṇe śarīraṃ pratiṣṭhitam | śarīre prāṇaḥ pratiṣṭhitaḥ | tadetadannamanne pratiṣṭhitam | sa ya etadannamanne pratiṣṭhitaṃ veda pratitiṣṭhati | ... || 1 ||
verses.trans: Do not speak ill of food. That is the sacred vow. The vital force is food, and the body is the eater of food. The body is established on the vital force; the vital force is established on the body. Thus food is established on food. He who knows this food established on food becomes firmly established.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that even though absolute realization has been taught, the Upanishad returns to practical, everyday discipline. Food must not be disrespected or despised, because matter is the outer manifestation of Brahman. The reciprocal relationship between body and breath reveals that the entire empirical universe is a beautifully interconnected web where everything supports everything else.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Food is treated as a direct blessing and physical expression of the Lord's mercy. Honoring food means honoring the Divine creative order that sustains the living body, allowing it to function as a vehicle for rendering devotional service.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that the vow to respect food is a direct command to respect the structural arrangements set up by Vishnu. The physical world is ordered by divine laws; observing these boundaries purifies the mind and deepens a soul's humility.

verses.sec: Bhṛguvallī — The Inquiry of Bhṛgu (Chapter III)
verses.dev: अन्नं न परिचक्षीत । तद्व्रतम् । आपो वा अन्नम् । ज्योतिरन्नादम् । अप्सु ज्योतिः प्रतिष्ठितम् । ज्योतिष्यापः प्रतिष्ठिताः । तदेतदन्नमन्ने प्रतिष्ठितम् । ... ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: annaṃ na paricakṣīta | tadvratam | āpo vā annam | jyotirannādam | apsu jyotiḥ pratiṣṭhitam | jyotiṣyāpaḥ pratiṣṭhitāḥ | tadetadannamanne pratiṣṭhitam | ... || 1 ||
verses.trans: Do not discard food. That is the sacred vow. Water is food, and fire is the eater of food. Fire is established on water; water is established on fire. Thus food is established on food. He who knows this food established on food becomes firmly established.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that water and fire are elements that consume and transform one another (lightning exists in rain clouds, water is produced by heat). Meditating on this mutual dependence teaches the mind to see through apparent differences, recognizing a singular elemental harmony operating across nature.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The matching of water and fire displays the organic synergy built into the cosmos by the Creator. No element is completely isolated; each works dependently with the others to form the single, living cosmic body of Brahman.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva highlights that the interactions between water and fire are supervised by specific deities acting under the direct, sovereign will of Vishnu, ensuring the universe remains balanced and habitable for living beings.

verses.sec: Bhṛguvallī — The Inquiry of Bhṛgu (Chapter III)
verses.dev: अन्नं बहु कुर्वीत । तद्व्रतम् । पृथिवी वा अन्नम् । आकाशोन्नादः । पृथिव्यामाकाशः प्रतिष्ठितः । आकाशे पृथिवी प्रतिष्ठिता । तदेतदन्नमन्ने प्रतिष्ठितम् । ... ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: annaṃ bahu kurvīta | tadvratam | pṛthivī vā annam | ākāśōnnādaḥ | pṛthivyāmākāśaḥ pratiṣṭhitam | ākāśe pṛthivī pratiṣṭhitā | tadetadannamanne pratiṣṭhitam | ... || 1 ||
verses.trans: Accumulate plenty of food. That is the sacred vow. The earth is food, and space is the eater of food. Space is established on earth; earth is established on space. Thus food is established on food. He who knows this food established on food becomes firmly established.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The command to 'produce plenty of food' is a social and spiritual duty. On a philosophical level, matching Earth and Space shows that the container (space) and the contained (earth) are completely interdependent, pointing back to the single consciousness that supports them both.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Generating abundance allows the householder to practice hospitality and charity, which are essential duties within the divine order. Earth and space are viewed as vast rooms within the Lord’s cosmic mansion, designed to nurture living souls.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets the accumulation of food as a practical step toward serving others and honoring the gifts of creation. The structural balance between space and earth is maintained by the independent power of Vishnu, keeping the planetary systems stable.

verses.sec: Bhṛguvallī — The Inquiry of Bhṛgu (Chapter III)
verses.dev: न कञ्चन वसतौ प्रत्याचक्षीत । तद्व्रतम् । तस्माद्यया कया च विध्यया बह्वन्नं प्राप्नुयात् । ... अहमनमहमन्नमहमन्नम् । अहमन्नादोऽहमन्नादोऽहमन्नादः । अहँ श्लोककृदहँ श्लोककृदहँ श्लोककृत् । अहमस्मि प्रथमजा ऋतस्य । ... य एवं वेद । इत्युपनिषत् ॥ १-६ ॥
verses.iast: na kañcana vasatau pratyācakṣīta | tadvratam | tasmādyayā kayā ca vidhyayā bahvannaṃ prāpnuyāt | ... ahamannamahamannamahamannam | ahamannādo'hamannādo'hamannādaḥ | ahã ślokakṛdahã ślokakṛdahã ślokakṛt | ahamasmi prathamajā ṛtasya | ... ya evaṃ veda | ityupaniṣat || 1-6 ||
verses.trans: Do not turn away anyone from your house. That is the sacred vow. Therefore, by whatever means possible, acquire plenty of food to share. ... [The realized sage breaks into a song of absolute liberation]: 'I am food! I am food! I am food! I am the eater of food! I am the eater of food! I am the eater of food! I am the poet who brings them together! I am the first-born of the divine order, prior to the gods, the center of immortality! He who gives me away protects me! I, who am food, consume the consumer of food! I have conquered the entire world! I am radiant like the sun!' He who knows this attains this realization. Thus ends the Upanishad.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The magnificent, ecstatic conclusion of the entire Upanishad. The non-dual sage, having dissolved all boundaries of the separate ego, experiences himself as the entire cosmic lifecycle. He is both the object (*Food*) and the subject (*the Eater*), as well as the unifying process (*the Song/Poet*). He realizes he is the primordial cosmic consciousness, existing prior to any specific names and forms, completely transcending the births and deaths of the relative world.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The closing song is an ecstatic expression of a liberated soul (*Mukta*) celebrating its total freedom and integration within the divine cosmos. The soul feels completely connected to all aspects of creation, rejoicing in its pristine nature while remaining filled with the supreme, loving bliss of the Almighty Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the liberated soul sings this song to praise the absolute, pervasive majesty of Vishnu. The statements 'I am food' and 'I am the eater' show that the soul sees the Lord's hand operating in every detail of the universe. The soul rejoices because it has been freed from material bondage, entering into an eternal state of conscious devotion, secure under the perfect, independent rule of Sri Hari.
